BIOS 333 — Week 1 In‑Class: Getting Comfortable with ggplot2

Lizbeth Macias

01 29, 26

  • 1 Goals for today (read this first)
  • 2 Required reading (for context, not a quiz)
  • 3 Setup
  • 4 Part A — The smallest possible ggplot
  • 5 Part B — Aesthetics: map vs set
  • 6 Part C — Break it on purpose, then fix it
  • 7 Part D — Layers: add a trend line, then modify it
  • 8 Part E — Facets: small multiples
  • 9 Part F — Labels, themes, and coordinate systems
  • 10 Part G — Final challenge (choose one)
  • 11 Turn‑in checklist
  • 12 Session info (required)

1 Goals for today (read this first)

By the end of class you should be able to:

  • Build a plot from scratch using ggplot() + layers (geoms).
  • Map variables to aesthetics (x, y, color, size, shape, alpha).
  • Debug common problems by breaking a plot on purpose and then fixing it.
  • Use themes and labels to improve readability.
  • Write short, clear notes that interpret what your code did and what your plot shows.

Important: This is an in‑class assignment. You must fill in the TODO sections, knit to HTML, and submit the HTML to Blackboard.

2 Required reading (for context, not a quiz)

You should have read (or skimmed) these before class:

  • Color basics: https://clauswilke.com/dataviz/color-basics.html
  • Aesthetic mapping: https://clauswilke.com/dataviz/aesthetic-mapping.html
  • Coordinate systems & axes: https://clauswilke.com/dataviz/coordinate-systems-axes.html
  • Healy (socviz) make a plot: https://socviz.co/makeplot.html#makeplot

3 Setup

3.1 0) Your notes (required)

In 3–5 bullet points, write what you think ggplot2 is doing when you make a plot.

TODO (write your notes below):

  • You tell ggplot which variables go where by mapping them to aesthetics like color, size, etc.
  • Themes and labels make the plot easier to read, without changing the data itself.
  • ggplot2 builds a plot in layers: you start with the data, then add pieces (like points or lines) one layer at a time.

3.2 1) Packages

We will use ggplot2 and a built‑in dataset (mtcars) so there is no data download needed.

# If you are running this on a computer that does NOT have ggplot2 installed yet,
# uncomment the next line once:
# install.packages("ggplot2")

library(ggplot2)

# Optional: for nicer data manipulation later (not required today)
# install.packages("dplyr")
# library(dplyr)

3.3 2) Load / inspect the dataset

mtcars is built into R. Let’s look at it.

# TODO: Run these lines and interpret what you see.

# View the first few rows
# head(mtcars)

# Look at column names
# names(mtcars)

# Get a quick summary
# summary(mtcars)

Interpretation (2–4 sentences):
TODO: What does one row represent? What are 2 variables you think might relate to fuel efficiency (mpg)?

  • One row represents one car are its characteristics measured since each column shows a unique feature like mpg or weight.

  • 2 variables I think might relate to fuel efficiency are weight (wt) since the heavier a vehicle is, the more fuel/gas it intakes, and size of the car since the larger the car, more fuel it needs.

4 Part A — The smallest possible ggplot

4.1 3) Start with a blank ggplot object

# TODO: Create a ggplot object that uses mtcars as the data.
# Hint: ggplot(data = mtcars)

# NOTE: removed hashtags in both p to run function properly. R ignores action if hashtag is in front of it.
p <- ggplot(data = mtcars)
p

Interpretation (1–2 sentences):
TODO: Why does this not show a plot yet?

  • This plot does not show a plot yet since no data has been given or instructed even though the command ran with no errors. We need to specify it.

4.2 4) Add points (a first geom)

# TODO: Add a scatterplot layer with geom_point()
# Requirements:
# - x axis: wt
# - y axis: mpg
# Note: removed hashtags on line ggplot and geom_point

ggplot(data = mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
geom_point()

Interpretation (2–4 sentences):
TODO: Describe the relationship between weight (wt) and miles per gallon (mpg). Is it positive or negative? Strong or weak?

  • The relationship between wt and mpg is negative because as a car’s weight increases, fuel efficiency tends to decrease when observing the graph. Additionally, the points show a clear downward trend, showing a strong trend.

5 Part B — Aesthetics: map vs set

3 Part B ### 5) Map a variable to color Note color point by number of cyinders

# TODO: Color points by number of cylinders (cyl)
# Hint: put color = cyl inside aes(...)
# Note: removed hashtages in ggplot and geom so that R doesn't ignore command.
 ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = cyl)) +
   geom_point()

#Note: make all points one color

Interpretation (2–4 sentences):
TODO: What does “mapping” mean here? What does the legend represent? - mapping means assigning a variable from the data (cyl) to create a an aesthetic for (color). Each point on the graph colors are determined by how many cylinders a particular car has. The legend shows which color goes to cylinder group it belongs in.

5.1 6) Set a constant color (NOT mapped)

# TODO: Make points blue (or any single color you like) BUT do not create a legend.
# Hint: put color = "blue" outside aes(...)
# Note: removed hashtags to make all plots all blue
 ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point(color = "blue")

Interpretation (1–2 sentences):
TODO: Explain why the previous plot had a legend but this one does not. - The previous plot had a legend because color was mapped to a variable (cyl) inside aes(), so ggplot needed a legend to explain the different colors. - In this plot, the color is set to a single value being (“blue”) that is outside aes(), so it does not represent any data. ### 7) Try other aesthetics

Choose ONE: - Map size to hp, OR - Map shape to gear, OR - Map alpha to disp

# TODO: Make points green (or any single color you like) BUT do not create a legend.
# Hint: put color = "green" outside aes(...)

 ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = cyl, shape = factor ( gear ))) + geom_point()

Interpretation (2–4 sentences):
TODO: Was your chosen aesthetic helpful or confusing? Why? - My chosen aesthetic was confusing since changing the shape to gear makes it visually difficult to understand what the data is interpreting even though a legend is present. —

6 Part C — Break it on purpose, then fix it

6.1 8) “Object not found” in ggplot2

Run the code below as is. You should get an error.

# DO NOT FIX THIS LINE YET — run it first and read the error.

#Note: added hashtags since it should give you an error but idn't want to have issues when knitting

# ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = weight, y = mpg)) +
#  geom_point()

TODO: Copy/paste the exact error message here:

Error in geom_point(): ! Problem while computing aesthetics. ℹ Error occurred in the 1st layer. Caused by error: ! object ‘weight’ not found Run rlang::last_trace() to see where the error occurred. >

Now fix it.

# TODO: Fix the plot by using the correct column name.
# Hint: check names(mtcars)
# Note: changed weight to x= wt because it doesn't know weight
# Your fixed code here
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point()

Interpretation (2–4 sentences):
TODO: What did the error mean in plain English? How did you diagnose it?

  • The “error” means that I asked R to use a variable/function, that it cannot locate in its current memory, in this case it couldn’t locate (wt) since it was written as “weight”.

7 Part D — Layers: add a trend line, then modify it

7.1 9) Add a smooth trend

Note: removed hashtags in lines 218 to 220

# TODO: Add geom_smooth() to your scatterplot of wt vs mpg
# Hint: add a new layer with +
# Note: a solid line and a surrounding grey area usually represent a regression line and its confidence interval produced by geom_smooth() function. 
 ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
   geom_point() +
   geom_smooth()
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'

7.2 10) Make the smooth line a linear model

# TODO: Modify geom_smooth so it fits a straight line.
# Hint: geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)
# Note: what does se: false do? turns confidence intervals off
# Note: added new code for smooth linear line
# Your code here
ggplot(mtcars, aes (x = wt, y= mpg)) +
   geom_point() +
   geom_smooth(method ="lm", se = FALSE)
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Interpretation (2–4 sentences):
TODO: What does se = FALSE change? Why might you turn it off? - ‘se = FALSE’ removes the shaded confidence interval ribbon around the regression line to make the plot look cleaner visually. —

8 Part E — Facets: small multiples

8.1 11) Compare groups with facets

# TODO: Facet by cylinders (cyl)
# Hint: + facet_wrap(~ cyl)
# Note: edit all this
# Your code here
ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, color = factor (cyl), shape = factor (gear))) + geom_point() +
   geom_smooth(method ="lm", se = FALSE) 
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

   facet_wrap(~cyl)
## <ggproto object: Class FacetWrap, Facet, gg>
##     attach_axes: function
##     attach_strips: function
##     compute_layout: function
##     draw_back: function
##     draw_front: function
##     draw_labels: function
##     draw_panel_content: function
##     draw_panels: function
##     finish_data: function
##     format_strip_labels: function
##     init_gtable: function
##     init_scales: function
##     map_data: function
##     params: list
##     set_panel_size: function
##     setup_data: function
##     setup_panel_params: function
##     setup_params: function
##     shrink: TRUE
##     train_scales: function
##     vars: function
##     super:  <ggproto object: Class FacetWrap, Facet, gg>

Interpretation (2–4 sentences):
TODO: In what way is faceting different from coloring? Which is clearer here? - faceting allows to plot in grids to show a different subset of data in a specific category. - coloring is more clearer when showing differences and direct comparisons while faceting prevents overlap of data points. —

9 Part F — Labels, themes, and coordinate systems

9.1 12) Make it readable (labels + theme)

# TODO: Improve your plot.
# Requirements:
# - Add a title
# - Label x and y axes with words (not just wt/mpg)
# - Use theme_minimal() OR theme_classic()

# Your code here
   ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, color = factor(cyl), shape = factor(gear))) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ cyl) +
  labs(
    title = "Fuel Efficiency vs Car Weight by Cylinder",
    x = "Car Weight (1000 lbs)",
    y = "Miles per Gallon (mpg)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Interpretation (2–4 sentences):
TODO: What design choices did you make and why? - Design choices I made to this graph is include a title on the top, title on both (x and y) axis. - This helped make the graph more visually appealing but also include the necessary information it needed to understand what the plot is interpreting. ### 13) Coordinate systems: zoom vs change scale

A. Zoom (keep the data, change what you see)

# TODO: Add coord_cartesian(ylim = c(10, 35)) (or similar)
# Use your favorite plot from above.
# Note: addded coord_cartesian as extended function in chunk. Not really sure what it did.
# Your code here
ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, color = factor(cyl), shape = factor(gear))) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ cyl) +
  labs(
    title = "Fuel Efficiency vs Car Weight by Cylinder",
    x = "Car Weight (1000 lbs)",
    y = "Miles per Gallon (mpg)"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(10, 35)) +
  theme_minimal()
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

B. Filter (remove data before plotting)

# TODO: Create a filtered dataset that keeps only cars with mpg <= 35 (or similar),
# then plot it. You may use base R or dplyr.

# Option 1 (base R):
# mt_small <- mtcars[mtcars$mpg <= 35, ]

# Option 2 (dplyr):
# mt_small <- mtcars %>% filter(mpg <= 35)
# Note: option 1: 32 observations empty_small of 11 variables
# Note: add $
# Your code here
# Note: did option 1

ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, color = factor(cyl), shape = factor(gear))) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ cyl) +
  labs(
    title = "Fuel Efficiency vs Car Weight by Cylinder",
    x = "Car Weight (1000 lbs)",
    y = "Miles per Gallon (mpg)"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(10, 35)) +
  theme_minimal()
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

  mt_small <- mtcars[mtcars$mpg <= 35, ]

ggplot(mt_small, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point()

Interpretation (3–5 sentences):
TODO: Explain the difference between zooming with coord_cartesian() and filtering the data first. Why does this matter ethically? - “Zooming” with coord_cartesian() restricts how we view the window of a plot while keeping the entire dataset intact - It acts as a magnifier. Examples could be trends, smoothers, etc. - “filtering” data first removes data points outside the specified range before plotting - This can change the statistical summaries and visual interpretations of the graphs created. This matters ethically because filtering unaware can lead to the removal of outliers or contextual information.

10 Part G — Final challenge (choose one)

Pick ONE challenge below and complete it. Your goal is to show you can build a plot, break it, and rebuild it.

10.1 Option 1: Boxplot + jitter

Note: add boxplot and jitter piece Note: geombox + jitter code Note: x=cylinder y=mpg Note: not doing geom or facet Make a boxplot of mpg by cyl, then overlay jittered points. # Note: included geom_boxplot and geom_jitter

# TODO: Your code here
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = factor(cyl), y = mpg)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.3)

Interpretation (2–4 sentences):
TODO: What does the boxplot summarize that the points do not (or vice versa)? - Boxplot summarizes and shows points in the median, quartiles and interquartile range (IQR). - In doing so, it provides immediate visual cues for overall spread and skewness, which can be difficult to tell without it.
